summary: The hip joint is a ball-and-socket (spheroidal) joint designed for stability over mobility.
The hip joint is a hinge joint designed primarily for mobility over stability.
The hip joint is a pivot joint that allows only rotation.
The acetabulum is formed by three bones: ilium (superior), ischium (posteroinferior), and pubis (anteroinferior).
The acetabulum is formed by two bones: ilium and ischium only.
The acetabulum is formed entirely by the ilium bone.
The iliofemoral ligament is the strongest ligament in the body and resists hyperextension and lateral rotation.
The ischiofemoral ligament is the strongest ligament in the body and resists flexion.
The pubofemoral ligament is the strongest ligament and resists medial rotation.
Femoral neck fractures risk avascular necrosis because the retinacular blood vessels from the medial circumflex femoral artery are damaged.
Femoral neck fractures risk avascular necrosis due to damage to the obturator artery only.
Femoral neck fractures have no risk of avascular necrosis as the blood supply is extensive.
In posterior dislocation of the hip, the limb is held in flexion, adduction, and internal rotation.
In posterior dislocation, the limb is held in extension, abduction, and external rotation.
In posterior dislocation, the limb is held in a neutral position with no deformity.
The sciatic nerve is at risk of injury during posterior dislocation of the hip joint.
The femoral nerve is at risk during posterior dislocation of the hip.
The obturator nerve is most commonly injured in posterior hip dislocation.
